title: AI recruited to speed up home approvals, in budget set to reshape housing market
full_text:
Artificial intelligence will be recruited to speed up assessments of housing and energy projects awaiting environmental approval, as the government races to build more homes and ward off a key frustration that is turning voters away from the major parties.
Tuesday's federal budget will include a $105.9 million commitment over four years to develop an AI tool to guide proponents and share environmental data, helping more projects get approved.
A half-billion-dollar package to implement environmental reforms that were passed last year also includes about $250 million to establish the National Environmental Protection Agency.
The commitment is the latest housing measure flagged for the federal budget, which is set to reshape Australia's housing market.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers said the AI assistance would bring on more supply.
"Our big productivity push is all about getting compliance costs down and cutting red tape, making it easier and faster to build, and making Australia a more attractive place to invest," Mr Chalmers said.
"This is a big investment that will make our economy more productive and will help to get crucial projects going in housing, energy and critical minerals."
Government grasps the nettle on housing
The government had already established a "strike team" to whittle down the backlog of homes awaiting approval, an effort the environment minister says has approved more than 20,000 homes since August last year, and will meet its target of 26,000 approvals by July.
Yesterday, the treasurer announced a $2 billion commitment to support the construction of a further 65,000 homes by funding the roads, water, power, and sewage connections necessary to open new lots.
And tax changes to be confirmed on Tuesday will wind back two major tax breaks for housing investors: the discount on capital gains tax, such as property sales, and the income tax deduction available to housing investors known as "negative gearing".
Labor's massive success at the 2025 federal election has given it the electoral cushion to attempt to break the back of a political issue that has dragged voters away from the major parties over successive elections.
Both the Greens and One Nation have drawn voter support over rising housing and property values that are beyond affordability, particularly among young people and first-home buyers.
Following the Liberal loss to One Nation in the Farrer by-election, Shadow Housing Minister Andrew Bragg said the major parties were getting what they deserved.
"We are where we deserve to be, over the last 10 years we haven't done enough policy work, and I think the generation of millennials, my generation, are pretty dirty on the major parties and with good reason," Senator Bragg said on Sunday.
"Most of the policies that have been turned out have been junk.
"Our job is to do a better job and actually to do the policy work… it's been about a decade since we've had a decent policy."
Labor begins to make case for another broken tax promise
The treasurer, who said in a National Press Club address soon after the 2025 election that he was preparing to "grasp the nettle" and wear some political pain to fix the budget, has flagged that it would include changes to make the housing market and tax system "fairer".
Changes to negative gearing and the capital gains (CGT) discount would be another significant breach of the faith of voters, following a broken promise last term not to amend a package of income tax cuts that would have mostly benefited higher-income earners.
Asked repeatedly on the election campaign trail whether the government would rule out changes to negative gearing or capital gains tax, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese responded with annoyance: "Yes. How hard is it? For the fiftieth time."
Mr Chalmers conceded on Sky News yesterday that Labor's election campaign focused "almost exclusively on supply".
The government has been accused of hiding its intentions during the most recent federal election to hit both tax perks.
Both policies were part of Labor's agenda at the 2019 election, and have become totems of the housing debate inside Labor as much as they have in the electorate.
After Labor's surprise defeat at that election, the policies were set aside.
But since the COVID-19 pandemic, house prices surged, migration swelled as borders reopened, and housing construction slowed in part due to supply chain issues, leading to a tightening in an already tight market — and inflaming the issue.
Mr Chalmers flagged on Sunday that the government was prepared to break its promises to address housing.
"If a government comes to a different view, then the onus is on the government, senior ministers like myself, in explaining why the government has come to a different view," Mr Chalmers said.
"In my case personally, it has become increasingly clear that all of our efforts on housing supply are very important, still the most important thing when it comes to dealing with issues in the housing market, but there are other issues as well."
The treasurer said that since the introduction of the 50 per cent CGT discount two decades ago, house prices had been decoupled from wages.
"Those are facts; I'm not pre-empting announcements on Tuesday night," Mr Chalmers said.
"But the housing market is not working for young people.
"It is locking out too many Australians, and we're not building enough homes … and so I think any responsible government like ours needs to take seriously the very genuine inter-generational concerns that people have and make the housing market fairer and make the tax system fairer as well."
Senator Bragg said it was "insane" to think the housing crisis could be solved with "higher taxes".
"When you've already collapsed housing supply, and you've got higher interest rates, now you have got the prospect of higher taxes in the budget," he said.
"Almost every economist says more tax is going to be bad for housing. Half the cost of a new house goes in government fees, charges and taxes, so why the hell would you consider putting a new tax on something that is already so heavily taxed?"
What do you want to see in the budget? How would it affect you?
summary: The Australian government is investing $105.9 million in AI to expedite environmental approvals for housing projects, alongside significant budget measures aimed at reshaping the housing market.
publication:

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: $105.9 million allocated for AI to assist in housing and energy project approvals.
$2 billion committed to support the construction of 65,000 homes.
Changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax discounts are expected in the upcoming budget.
relevance: These developments may impact housing market dynamics and investment strategies, which are critical considerations for actuaries and financial services professionals.
